data.title: Stock index futures tumble as Trump's tariffs stoke global trade war concerns
data.description:
Stock index futures tumbled on Thursday, and yields plunged as President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs ignited fears of a potential global trade war. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -3.7%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -4.1%, and Dow futures (INDU) -2.8%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 11 basis points to 4.08%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 8 basis points to 3.78%.

Donald Trump detailed new tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports. The minimum baseline tariff will be 10%, and the highest rate for the latest round of tariffs is 49%.

"In short, the tariffs put in place last night were extraordinary both in terms of scale and in how they were calculated, with President Trump announcing reciprocal tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) as he declared a national emergency over the trade deficit," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

Markets have seen a strong risk-off reaction to the tariff announcement, with S&P futures down, which would bring the index back into correction territory if it materializes in the regular session today, Reid added. 

"Trump has tended to retreat rapidly from tariffs that visibly hurt U.S. consumers. Having a 10% near-universal tariff questions how far retreats go. If there is no retreat, markets will price a US recession. If there is a retreat, markets will assume U.S. growth will weaken," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The direct effect of such taxes raises U.S. price levels by 1.7% to 2.2%—the change in effective tariff rates multiplied by the share of imports. Second-round effects matter. Will U.S. manufacturers raise prices? Will U.S. retailers indulge in profit-led inflation? A 10% tariff increase means around a 4% consumer price increase, but retailers may use the narrative to raise prices more, Donovan added. 

Wall Street's major averages had, however, finished higher on Wednesday, ahead of Trump's announcement of the plan to impose reciprocal tariffs on imports coming into the U.S.

Initial jobless claims unexpectedly fell in the past week. The federal government led job cuts for March.

The U.S. trade deficit narrowed slightly less than expected in February.

The March ISM services index and the March PMI composite final will also land today. The former is expected to come in at 53, and the latter is expected at 53.5.   

The Fed balance sheet will come in later in the day.

data.title: TikTok U.S. gets bid from OnlyFans founder's startup; app set to be fined for GDPR breach
data.description:
OnlyFans founder Tim Stokely's startup has submitted an eleventh-hour bid along with a cryptocurrency foundation to buy TikTok U.S., according to a Reuters report from Wednesday, joining the likes of e-commerce and tech giant Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), which also threw its hat in the ring for the short video content platform.

Stokely's startup Zoop and the Hbar Foundation, which manages the Hedera crypto network's treasury, sent an "intent to bid" this week to the White House, the report said.

"Our bid for TikTok isn't just about changing ownership; it's about creating a new paradigm where both creators and their communities benefit directly from the value they generate," Zoop co-founder RJ Phillips reportedly told Reuters.

Phillips said his partners have been working with a consortium of investors but did not name them, nor did he provide details of the bid.

Media reports also suggested that mobile technology company AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) is looking to make a bid for TikTok U.S. and is in talks with the Trump administration.

EU GDPR Breach

Separately, a Bloomberg report from Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter, said the app's Beijing-based parent, ByteDance (BDNCE), is expected to be slapped with a €500M+ fine before the end of the month by Ireland’s data protection commission for illegally sending European users’ data to China.

The report, citing people who spoke on condition of anonymity, said the app was in breach of the European Union’s General Data Protection Regulation by sending the information to China to be accessed by engineers. The exact size of TikTok’s fine and the timing of the decision aren’t final and could still change, they said.

data.title: Guess? drops premarket as special committee evaluating WHP offer retains legal advisors
data.description:
• Guess? (NYSE:GES) shares dropped over 7% premarket on Thursday, the same day it announced that its special committee, formed to consider the non-binding takeover offer from WHP Global, has retained its financial and legal advisors.
  • Solomon Partners will continue as the financial advisor and Willkie Farr & Gallagher as legal advisor to the special committee.
  • The apparel brand has an offer to be acquired for $13 per share by WHP Global, representing a 34% premium to the stock closing price on March 14.
  • The company’s special committee is still evaluating the offer with its financial and legal advisors and has not yet determined whether to agree to the offer or pursue any other transaction.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Apple falls after Trump's tariffs kick in; BofA expects company to manage supply chain to minimize impact
data.description:
Update: The story was updated with TF International Securities' Analyst Ming-Chi Kuo's post, and BofA analysts' note. 

Shares of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) tumbled about 8% premarket on Thursday after President Donald Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs were announced on trading partners including China, where the iPhone maker has a large manufacturing presence.

BofA Securities reiterated its Buy rating on Apple, citing resilient margins, cash flows and capitalreturns but lowered the price target on the stock to $250 from $265. The firm noted that China risk is more real, but several outcomes are possible.

Analysts led by Wamsi Mohan said given the news on tariffs, all of Apple’s products will be subject to tariffs at various rates. They expect Apple to manage its supply chain to minimize the impact, but if the tariff rates stand and Apple absorbs the entire $20B of headwind and 500 basis points to gross margins, they expect an impact of $1.24 to EPS in C2026.

The analysts noted that despite the tariff headwinds, they view the 15% potential cut to EPS (without offsets) as attractive compared to the potential global disruption which could be caused by an escalating trade war and view Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) as relatively defensive.

Mohan and his team said that while the risk of further trade escalation remains, there is also a chance that things could end up better than feared and Apple could use several mitigating strategies including — raising prices on products (in the U.S. and/or globally); pressuring supply chain even more; raising pricing on Services; appeal for exceptions; reoptimize supply chain; and  potentially move away from a cadence of annual product releases.

In addition, the analysts said they have previously estimated that it would cost about 20% more to make an iPhone in the U.S. compared to China. Increased automation and walking away from timed annual product releases to that which are more spread out throughout the year would put less pressure on peak to trough labor costs, according to the analysts.

If all the logistics were theoretically dealt with and some compromises made, it is conceptually possible to move production to the U.S. However, components would still be sourced from Asia for the foreseeable future, the analysts added.

Meanwhile, TF International Securities' Analyst Ming-Chi Kuo said if Apple keeps prices unchanged, its overall gross margin could significantly drop by an estimated 8.5% to 9%. He added that India and Vietnam are far more likely than China to secure U.S. tariff exemptions. The timeline is unclear but this would speed up Apple’s shift of assembly orders away from China until non-Chinese manufacturing can satisfy most U.S. demand.

Kuo said in a post on X that by 2025, at least 15% of global iPhone production is expected to move to India, up from the 10% to 12% in 2024. Given China’s unlikely concessions to the U.S. for tariff exemptions, Apple could reduce the gross margin hit to 5.55 to 6% without raising prices if India and Vietnam secure tariff waivers through new U.S. agreements. If India secures tariff exemptions and Apple boosts its iPhone manufacturing capacity there to over 30% of the global supply, the negative impact on gross margins could decline to just 1% to 3%, according to the analyst.

On Wednesday, Trump announced his tariff plans, which would impact key nations in the semiconductor supply chain, including 34% on China, 32% on Taiwan and 24% on Japan.

"Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), which has built a lot in China, is going to spend $500B to build plants here," Trump said on Wednesday while announcing the tariff plans.

Apple did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Besides manufacturing in China, Apple also depends upon Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) for supplying advanced chips.

India — where Apple is bolstering its manufacturing base as it seeks to diversify beyond China — was hit with a 26% tariff. Apple's Taiwan-based manufacturing partner Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF), reportedly, plans to make about 25M to 30M iPhones at its facilities in India this year, over double the number it made last year.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Intercontinental Exchange reports 23% higher Q1 average daily volume
data.description:
• Intercontinental Exchange (NYSE:ICE) announced on Thursday a 23% year-over-year rise in the first-quarter average daily volume.
  • Energy ADV increased by 24% from last year. Oil ADV was up 18%, natural Gas ADV 33%, and Environmentals ADV 15%.
  • Sugar ADV rose by 20% on a yearly basis.
  • Financials ADV increased by 28%, NYSE Cash Equities ADV by 20%, and NYSE Equity Options ADV by 8%.
  • "The support from our customers has created the depth of liquidity that our markets offer, and we thank them for making the first quarter of 2025 the highest traded volume quarter in ICE’s history," said President Ben Jackson.

data.title: Mitsubishi may consider investing in Alaska LNG project, CEO says
data.description:
Mitsubishi (OTCPK:MSBHF) may consider investing in a liquefied natural gas project in Alaska, although any decision will require careful review, CEO Katsuya Nakanishi said Thursday while unveiling plans to spend at least ¥4T (~$27B) over the next three years.

"We have been approached and are considering it," Nakanishi told reporters when asked about possible participation in the Alaska LNG project.

Mitsubishi (OTCPK:MSBHF) will evaluate the project's feasibility, considering factors such as the 800-mile distance to the U.S. west coast, the need to build liquefaction facilities, and future LNG demand in Southeast Asia and other regions.

Alaskan officials have said the project could attract U.S. investors for most of the pipeline part, while any equity from Asian buyers likely would be tied to offtake from the liquefaction plant that will prepare the gas for export.

Glenfarne recently agreed to acquire a 75% ownership stake in the potential project, according to the Alaska Gasline Development Corporation, the state agency that now holds a 25% stake through its 8 Star Alaska subsidiary.

data.title: Smith-Midland awarded $5M SlenderWall contract for FBI project
data.description:
• Smith-Midland (NASDAQ:SMID) on Thursday said it has been selected to produce more than $5M of SlenderWall and architectural precast concrete panels for existing dormitories at the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) in Quantico, Virginia.
  • Production of SlenderWall, Smith-Midland's high-performance, lightweight precast concrete cladding system, is set to begin in June 2025 at the company's Virginia plant, with installation slated for October 2025.
  • SMID -4.09% premarket to $30.0.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: CrowdStrike appoints chief technology innovation officer
data.description:
• Cybersecurity company CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) (NEOE:CRWD:CA) announced on Thursday the appointment of Alex Ionescu as chief technology innovation officer.
  • Shares were -4.51% pre-market at $354.75.
  • The company's founding chief architect and former vice president of endpoint engineering has rejoined in the current role.

data.title: Quant Comparison: Mag7 scores high on profitability, valuations remain a concern
data.description:
The Magnificent 7, which represents about a third of the benchmark S&P 500, has taken a beating this year. 

Sentiments around the growth stocks turned sour even before President Trump launched a trade war, stoking macroeconomic uncertainty and raising recession risks in the U.S, but tariff actions have sent the group even lower.

The collective also lost a whopping $15T in market cap after the launch of China’s DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) R1 open-source AI model back in January, while the Mag7 also accounts for nearly the entire downturn in the Nasdaq 100 YTD. 

The group now trades near levels just prior to the 2020 presidential election, surrendering most of the "animal spirit" gains.

As near-term risk remains apparent for the Magnificent Seven, Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings points out the visible strengths of this group. 

On the profitability front, SA Quant gave the highest possible grades to the group as a whole. Meanwhile, when considering growth prospects stocks like Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) outperformed others like Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL). 

Valuations, however, proved to be a bane for the group. SA Quant gave most stocks the lowest possible rating, while Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) fared better. 

Mag7 also showed strong momentum, with most stocks performing well on this front. However, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) were the exceptions, scoring a C, the lowest among the bunch. 

Coming to EPS revisions, Mag7 saw varied grades. Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) earned the highest score amongst the bunch, while Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) got the lowest grade among the bunch at D+. 

Seeking Alpha grades each stock by five “factors” -- Value, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions. For more details, see Quant Ranking and Factor Grades FAQ.

data.title: Papa Johns and Google Cloud team up to deliver AI-powered pizza experiences
data.description:
• Google Cloud (NASDAQ:GOOG) Thursday reported an expanded multi-year partnership with Papa Johns (NASDAQ:PZZA) to transform the pizza maker's ordering and delivery experience using artificial intelligence.
  • Through the partnership, Papa Johns has created an innovation team, named PJX, that will leverage Google Cloud's (AI), data analytics and machine learning capabilities.
  • Papa Johns will also be able to predict customer ordering patterns to provide relevant promotions and ordering shortcuts.
